column36: Contents include but are not limited to church activates A.M.E. services, Ep worth League disputes over race, missions, Sunday-school concerts, social events and clubs, and civic matters including employment of Black men in police, fire, and city work. It editorializes on race relations, miscegenation laws, press bias against Black people, and Republican politics in Iowa, praising unity and fair nominations. National and international briefs cover the China-Japan peace, Mexican cattle importation to check beef prices, Cuban rebellion, income tax legal questions and other headlines train robberies, judicial cases, industrial strikes. Local items include arrests, a stabbing at the poor farm, charities, lectures, patent notices, business ads, and social gatherings. The paper mixes news, moral commentary, evangelism, and community organizing aimed at uplifting and protecting Black citizens' interests.

column36: Contents include but not limited to extensive local and national news reports. Des Moines community notes on church services, Sunday school parade disputes, social entertainments and club elections, a plea for colored voter attention, and a list of civic leaders. Crime reports describe assaults, a lynching attempt, an unresolved child death investigation, and murder trials. Transportation coverage recounts a fast train wreck near Waterloo with multiple injuries, a steamer collision and sinking, and railroad sales. Weather and agriculture items report heavy rains, ample soil moisture, good crop prospects, and a destructive cyclone that killed and injured many. Miscellany covers patent practice, legal proceedings, funerals, and international incidents, and other topics.

Porter travels to Memphis to represent Northern Congregationalists. Jordan, attended by prominent Des Moines society and featuring military honors. Obituaries include Mrs. D. Roy and Robert Raikes, whose funeral drew pastors from multiple churches. Clubs such as the Calanan Industrial held meetings, while Sioux City reported new pastoral work and lectures on the “Race Problem.” Editorials highlight President Roosevelt’s Mississippi Valley trip and growth of the Metropolitan Mercantile & Realty Co., promoting Black enterprise. A major fire destroyed Buxton’s public school, though classes resumed at alternate sites. Obituaries include Mrs. Daniel Roy of Des Moines and Mr. Washington Graham of Burlington. Rev. T.L. Griffith reports on the Metropolitan Mercantile and Realty Company’s progress in Baltimore, highlighting Black business enterprise and property development, and other topics.

The Afro-American Council planned a debate on immigration as a solution to the race problem. A profile of Lee Welker, the first Black graduate of Yale from Iowa, was celebrated. National news notes Bishop Potter honoring Bishop Ferguson of Africa, and the death of Hon. John L. Waller, and other topics.

The Negro Lyceum debated Washington vs. Du Bois, siding with Washington. National and international reports highlight Bishop Samuel Ferguson’s work in Africa and Booker T. Washington’s essay on the Negro in northern cities. Editorials emphasize patriotism, homeownership, and moral uplift, and other topics.

Reports note the sudden death of Gus Saunders in Colorado and Isaac Johnson in Keokuk, along with other obituaries. Community updates cover new church openings, farm ventures in South Dakota, and large land purchases by wealthy Black farmers from Georgia, and other topics.

The Bystander announced plans for its unique holiday edition. National reports feature the Pittman Washington wedding at Tuskegee Institute and a planned Race Conference at the Jamestown Exposition, and other topics.

National reports feature Andrew Carnegie’s lecture on “The Negro in America” in Edinburgh, praising racial progress, and news of Howard University’s anniversary in Washington, D.C. The Negro Republican Club elected officers, while social notes cover travel, weddings, and a musical program by McDowell’s orchestra. column34: Ruff, Charles S.;Ruff, Thaddeus S.;Thompson, John L. (John Lay), 1869-1930
column36: Contents include but not limited to a special feature on Buxton, Iowa, highlighting its rapid growth as a coal-mining town with a majority African American population. The article describes the town’s clean design, absence of saloons, and modern housing. It praises the Consolidation Coal Company for employing thousands of Black miners and supporting community welfare. The Monroe Mercantile Company’s massive general store, staffed partly by African Americans, is profiled as one of the most advanced in the Midwest. Leaders like L.E. Johnson and LeRoy Tucker of the Y.M.C.A. are celebrated for fostering Christian values and youth development, and other topics.

Porter lectured on Lincoln, McKinley, and Douglas, and prepared a sermon on George Washington. A debate on Japanese naturalization was announced by the David and Jonathan League. Political columns feature B.F. Loos’ candidacy for mayor, Wesley Ash’s bid for commissioner, and tributes to Judge D. Augustus Straker of Detroit, and other topics.

Lyons. Rev. J.W. Harris of Burns M.E. gave his farewell sermon before moving to conference. A major debate between the Des Moines Lyceum and St. Joseph’s Douglass Society was held on repealing the 15th Amendment, with strong participation. The Intellectual Improvement Club elected delegates to the State Federation in Buxton. Fred Jackson, veteran fireman and Mason, hosted a grand reception, while a new business, the Variety Co., was launched by L.H.S. Brown and R.E. Patton. The Negro Republican Club prepared voters under the new Des Moines Plan, and editorials discussed the Brownsville Affair and upcoming city elections, and other topics.

Reid of Sioux City praised Black advancement in Mobile, Alabama, despite Jim Crow. Community activities included the Union Congregational picnic, the Negro Lyceum’s debate, and upcoming rallies at Burns M.E. and A.M.E. churches. Pettigrew lectured on the Colored Cooperative League. Grand Lodge, and a lawn fête with the Capital City Band was announced at the A.M.E. Church. Obituaries include Mr. Thomas McDaniels, once Des Moines’ wealthiest Black citizen. Senator William B. Elliston, founder of the Iowa Federation of Colored Women’s Clubs, returned from Kentucky, praised for her musical and civic work. The issue strongly condemned the Springfield, Illinois race riot and reprinted Rev. Cornelius Reid’s sermon denouncing mob violence. Dr. Griffin of the National Rescue Association visited to establish a local rescue home. The Negro Lyceum discussed “Negroes in Music,” featuring Prof. Warrick and Miss Lillian Fields. Editor John L. Thompson described his 10-county Iowa trip, noting rural prosperity and Black professionals in Omaha. Political columns detailed the Republican State Central Committee’s new headquarters and conference speeches by Cummins and Dolliver, and other topics.

Editorials condemned a brutal assault in the city and urged strong moral action. Political columns supported Governor Cummins as Allison’s Senate successor and endorsed Judge W.H. McHenry for the Supreme Court. Reports from Burlington, Sioux City, and St. Joseph highlighted church events, community improvements, and notable local figures, and other topics.

Editorials condemned the Springfield, Illinois race riot and praised the grand jury indictments of over 100 rioters. Numerous social gatherings, weddings, and club meetings filled the week, including luncheons for visiting guests from Keosauqua and Macon. The Negro Lyceum discussed John Greenleaf Whittier’s writings and planned a public program at St. Paul A.M.E. Editorials demanded fair city employment for Black citizens under the Des Moines Plan and urged loyalty to the Republican Party. National items covered major Negro conventions, and other topics.

Curtis’s essay “The Negro in the South.” Regional reports from Clarinda, Burlington, Dubuque, Davenport, and Macon covered church work, obituaries, and political speeches, and other topics.

Barton returned to Washington after a farewell reception, and the Negro Lyceum planned declamatory contests. Obituaries included Miss Ida Strothers of Omaha. Reports from Burlington and Mt.
